reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that $_GET['formkey'] is assigned to $this->formkey at line 32 without sanitize_key() or sanitize_text_field(), which violates WordPress coding standards. However, the claimed exploitability is refuted by the code. At line 36, a strict in_array(..., true) whitelist check compares $this->formkey against the return value of MW_WP_Form_Contact_Data_Setting::get_form_post_types(), which is the set of registered WP post type slugs for the plugin. If the value is not an exact string match to one of those slugs, the constructor calls exit immediately. WordPress post type slugs are constrained to alphanumeric characters, underscores, and hyphens at registration — they cannot contain injection payloads. No attacker-controlled value can reach the sinks at line 119 (option name concatenation) or line 124 (admin_url argument) unless it is already a valid, registered, benign post type slug from the database. Additionally, the _save() path (which reaches line 119) is additionally gated by check_admin_referer() at line 111, providing a second blocker. The redirect at line 122-126 uses wp_redirect() which produces an HTTP Location header, not HTML output, so there is no reflected XSS vector even in the redirect path. The absence of sanitize_key() is a coding standard deficiency but not an exploitable vulnerability because the strict whitelist exit-on-mismatch is a complete blocker for the injection chain described.
